column1: [Ch 01 • Recall 1] State the fundamental problem of causal inference in one sentence.
column2: For every unit, we observe one of two potential outcomes; the other is missing. (Equivalently: we observe $Y_i(D_i)$ and never the other one.)

column1: [Ch 01 • Recall 2] Why does randomisation solve the selection-bias problem?
column2: Because randomisation severs the link between treatment status $D_i$ and the potential outcomes $Y_i(0), Y_i(1)$. In expectation, treated and untreated units have the same untreated potential outcome, so the bracketed selection-bias term in the decomposition collapses to zero, and the naive comparison estimates the ATE without bias. It does not equalise the two groups on every realisation; it equalises them in expectation.

column1: [Ch 01 • Recall 3] In your paper's setting, what is the (impossible) experiment that would directly answer the central question?
column2: Pick a country (say the UK). Hold its workers, labour market, electoral history, and demographic composition fixed. Replace *only* its welfare design with Norway's (or some other high-CWED design). Observe the slope of RTI on anti-immigration before and after the swap. The difference is the causal effect of welfare design on the RTI-to-exclusion conversion, holding everything else constant. This is impossible because welfare designs are not detachable from the institutional histories that produced them.

column1: [Ch 01 • Recall 4] Given that experiment is impossible, what's your paper's substitute, and what does it cost you?
column2: The substitute is a cross-sectional comparison across 15 countries that already vary in welfare design, modelling within-country slopes of RTI on anti-immigration and asking whether between-country slope variation tracks between-country welfare design variation. The cost is that the 15 countries differ in many things besides welfare design; the substitute conflates the welfare-design effect with anything else that varies cross-nationally. Macro-controls partly address this; the within-country thesis design will address it more cleanly.

column1: [Ch 01 • Recall 5] Suppose your paper's r=−0.85 had come out instead at r=−0.10, p=0.71. Would that have been evidence for the *symmetric* buffering account, or evidence for "no welfare-design effect at all", or something else? Be precise.
column2:
It would have been evidence for "no detectable welfare-design effect at this scale of variation, given this slope-extraction methodology". It would *not* by itself be evidence for the symmetric account, because the symmetric account predicts a positive correlation in the *other* direction (more spending → less exclusion would show up as a different sign). r=−0.10 with high p-value would be a wash — consistent with no effect, with a small effect masked by sample noise, with measurement issues, or with the pattern being too small to detect at N=15. To distinguish "small true effect" from "no true effect" you would need either a much larger N or a much sharper measurement, neither of which observational welfare-state data offers easily. The asymmetric mechanism's empirical content lives in its predictions being *strong enough* to be seen at N=15. If they hadn't been, the theoretical claim would still be live but the empirical case would be much weaker.

If you got this right, you have understood that "no result" and "result against the theory" are different things, and that small samples do not give you the power to distinguish them cleanly. Hold onto this thought; it returns in Chapter 18 when we discuss reading nulls as evidence.

column1: [Ch 01 • Try-this 1] Without reading further, write down (in one sentence each) the analogue of this two-classroom story for your welfare-state paper. Specifically: what is the "your class vs my class" comparison, and what is the missing potential outcome you cannot observe?
column2: The "your class vs my class" comparison is between two welfare states with different decommodification levels — say, the UK (low CWED, ≈28) and Norway (high CWED, ≈43). The observed comparison is "British workers exposed to RTI become more anti-immigration than Norwegian workers exposed to the same RTI". The missing potential outcome is what British high-RTI workers *would* have looked like if Britain had Norway's welfare design — same workers, same labour market shock, different welfare encounter. We can never observe that. Your whole paper is a careful attempt to construct a credible substitute by triangulating across 15 countries and exploiting variation in CWED. (If you wrote something close to this you are already ahead of where most MSc students are.)

column1: [Ch 01 • Try-this 2] Suppose I tell you that countries with higher welfare generosity tend to have populations that were already more cosmopolitan and less hostile to immigration before any welfare reforms happened. If you compare anti-immigration attitudes across welfare regimes naively, in which direction does selection bias push the apparent effect of welfare generosity? Toward "welfare reduces hostility" or toward "welfare increases hostility"?
column2:
Toward "welfare reduces hostility". High-CWED countries had lower untreated $Y(0)$ (less baseline hostility) for reasons that have nothing to do with the welfare state's causal effect. The naive comparison would give you a *more* negative welfare-attitudes correlation than the true causal effect warrants. Selection bias and treatment effect run in the same direction here, and you cannot separate them from the comparison alone.

This is exactly why §V.G of your paper says "Country-level welfare indicators are confounded with other institutional differences". You are naming the selection-bias term explicitly.

column1: [Ch 01 • Try-this 3] Suppose your colleague secretly switched two of her students for two of yours, picking the strongest of hers and the weakest of yours. (a) What does this do to selection bias in the naive comparison of class averages? (b) What does it do if you re-randomise *after* the swap?
column2: (a) Selection bias is now non-zero in a specific direction: your class is weaker on average (you lost a strong one, gained a weak one); her class is stronger on average. The naive comparison will *underestimate* the effect of your teaching. (b) Re-randomisation eliminates the bias. The post-swap composition is now balanced in expectation again. This is why "randomisation" is sometimes called the "great equaliser" — it doesn't undo any specific imbalance, it makes the *expected* imbalance zero.

column1: [Ch 01 • Try-this 4] For each of the following claims from your paper, name which of the four moves above it is implicitly relying on (or which one it would need but cannot make). (a) "RTI predicts anti-immigration attitudes after controlling for age, gender, education, income, and urban residence." (b) "Welfare decommodification accounts for 72 per cent of the cross-country variation in RTI-slopes." (c) "The Danish 2003 activation reforms produced damage signatures with decommodification held constant."
column2:
(a) **Conditioning** (move 1). The covariates are functioning as the $X$ in "treatment is as-good-as-random within $X$-cells". Whether that's defensible depends on whether you've conditioned on enough things; the answer is "probably not for full causal identification but enough for a defensible attitudinal-correlation finding".

(b) **None of the above, cleanly.** This is a *between-country* descriptive statistic. You cannot make a causal claim from it without invoking move (1) — that the 15 countries are comparable conditional on observables. Your paper is honest about this in §V.G ("country-level welfare indicators are confounded with other institutional differences"). The cross-level interaction in Model 3 is a partial-conditioning version that does some but not enough work.

(c) **Differencing** (move 2). This is your thesis design speaking, not your seminar paper. You exploit *within-country* variation across reform episodes, holding the country fixed. The before-after-reform difference within each individual identifies the effect of the reform on that individual's attitudes (subject to its own assumptions, which we'll cover in Chapter 22).

If you got the third one right, you have already internalised the central architectural difference between your seminar paper and your thesis. That difference is the entire reason the thesis can claim things the seminar cannot.

column1: [Ch 01 • Defence 1] A methodologically aggressive referee says: "Your r=−0.85 is just confounding. Sweden has Sweden's welfare state and Sweden's politics; you cannot separate the two from cross-sectional data." You have 30 seconds. What do you say?
column2:
"You're right that I cannot separate them with cross-sectional data alone, and §V.G says so directly. What I can show is that the cross-country pattern is consistent with the asymmetric mechanism *and* inconsistent with the symmetric buffering account that has dominated this literature. Two things sharpen that consistency: (1) the macro-controls robustness rules out the most obvious confounders (GDP growth, inequality), and (2) the asymmetric prediction extends to a *null* on the redistribution side (β=0.011, p=0.29), which the symmetric account cannot generate. The next step is the within-country Danish design that is robust to time-invariant institutional confounding by construction. The seminar paper is a consistency check on a theoretical claim; the thesis is the identification."

The point is not to claim more than the design supports. The point is to be precise about what the design DOES support, which is "consistency with the asymmetric mechanism on multiple independent margins, in a way the symmetric account cannot match". That is a real intellectual contribution even when it isn't yet a clean causal estimate.

column1: [Walkthrough • Concept 1: Multilevel models with random slopes] How do you defend this choice in 30 seconds?
column2: The random-slopes specification is required by the data — the LR test rejects slope homogeneity at p < 10⁻²⁰. Ignoring slope variation would understate the SE on the cross-level interaction by a factor of about 3.6 (we get SE = 0.024 with random slopes vs SE = 0.0067 with random intercepts only on the same complete-case sample). The wider SE under random slopes is the correct SE; the smaller one is buying its precision by assuming the slopes are identical across countries, which is exactly what we're trying to test against. We pair this with OLS + country-wave fixed effects + clustered SEs as a robustness check, and the substantive coefficient is directionally identical.

column1: [Walkthrough • Concept 2: Cross-level interactions] How do you defend this choice in 30 seconds?
column2: Model 3's cross-level interaction is the principled test of whether welfare context moderates the conversion of RTI into anti-immigration. β₃ = −0.059, p = 0.015 says: each one-SD increase in CWED reduces the RTI-slope by 0.059 scale points. We could have run separate regressions per regime, but that produces five slopes with no formal test of whether they differ from each other and no efficient pooling of information. The cross-level interaction gets us one parameter, one p-value, and the right standard error — which is wider than naive OLS would give precisely because it's identified off cross-country slope variation, of which we have a finite (15-country) amount.

column1: [Walkthrough • Concept 3: Likelihood ratio tests] How do you defend this choice in 30 seconds?
column2: We use the LR test rather than AIC/BIC because for nested random-effects structures the LR test gives a probability statement under a null hypothesis. The χ²(2) statistic for our random-slopes-vs-random-intercepts comparison exceeds 90, which puts the p-value below 10⁻²⁰. That's not a marginal call. AIC and BIC would also overwhelmingly favour random slopes here; the LR test is just the convention, and it's the one most referees expect to see for this kind of nested model comparison. We refit both models with ML rather than REML for the test itself, since REML log-likelihoods aren't comparable across different fixed/random structures.

column1: [Walkthrough • Concept 4: Cluster-robust standard errors] How do you defend this choice in 30 seconds?
column2: Within a country-wave, observations share unobserved shocks — electoral campaigns, media environments, contemporaneous economic conditions — that create correlation in residuals that the individual-level controls cannot fully absorb. With cluster sizes around 1,400 individuals and modest intra-cluster correlation around 0.05, the design effect is 71, which means naive SEs are 8× too small. Cluster-robust SEs correct this without requiring distributional assumptions. We pair them with country-wave fixed effects: the dummies absorb the level shifts deterministically, the clustering handles the residual within-cluster correlation. With G = 136 clusters we're well above the small-sample threshold where cluster-robust starts misbehaving.

column1: [Walkthrough • Concept 5: The matched-sample logic and the BLUPs question] How do you defend this choice in 30 seconds?
column2: The §V.D scatter plots BLUPs from a random-slopes mixed model with individual-level controls — that's the most efficient slope estimator available, and it has two properties that matter for this paper. First, it partials out within-country confounders (RTI workers are systematically older and lower-income, both of which independently predict anti-immigration). Second, it shrinks toward a common grand mean, which protects against any single small-N country dominating the figure. The matched-sample comparison with ALMP uses the same BLUPs methodology on both sides, so when ALMP gives $r = 0.01$ and CWED gives $r = -0.86$ on the same 15 countries with the same estimator, the difference is about what the welfare measures capture, not about how the slopes were computed. We can show the bivariate version too as a robustness check; it gives a weaker $r = -0.63$, which is still significant, but the BLUPs spec is the principled choice.

column1: [Walkthrough • Concept 6: Inference at N=15] How do you defend this choice in 30 seconds?
column2: N=15 is small but not pathological for cross-national welfare-state research. Three things make the inference defensible. First, single-country jackknife on BLUPs: the correlation moves from r=−0.85 across all 15 countries to a worst case of r=−0.79 when any one country is removed. Never crosses zero, never loses statistical significance. Second, two-country jackknife across all 105 pairs: the worst-case removal (UK + Norway, the two endpoints) gives r=−0.71 with p=0.006 — softer, but still substantial and still significant. Third, the individual-level cross-level interaction (Model 3) is identified from N=82,000 individuals and gives β=−0.059 with p=0.015, which is a more powerful test of the same hypothesis. The within-country thesis design will replace the 15-country comparison with a far larger number of within-country before-after comparisons; until then, the triangulation across all three checks is what you can defend.

column1: [Walkthrough • Concept 7: The two-DV asymmetry] How do you defend this choice in 30 seconds?
column2: The solidarity null is not a measurement failure; it's a substantive finding the asymmetric theory predicts. Three things make this defensible. First, the same data, same estimator, comparable specification picked up the exclusion moderation cleanly (β = −0.059, p = 0.015), so the data has plenty of power to detect moderation when it exists. Power to detect a solidarity moderation of equal magnitude exceeds 0.99. Second, the ISSP supplementary test on a different sample, different outcome, different time period gives the same null (β = +0.010, p = 0.55) — two independent failures with completely different measurement instruments. Third, the asymmetric mechanism in §III predicts the null on three separate grounds: loss aversion, the positional nature of status, and the irreversibility of defensive identity investments. A theory that predicts the null and observes the null is doing exactly what theories are supposed to do.

column1: [Walkthrough • Concept 8: The behavioural-vs-attitudinal distinction] How do you defend this choice in 30 seconds?
column2: Attitudes are what voters are; votes are what voters do given supply-side constraints. The attitude moderation in Model 2 (RTI × Liberal = +0.127, p = 0.003) tests whether welfare context shapes the conversion of vulnerability into exclusionary disposition; the answer is yes. The radical right voting moderation in Model 6 (RTI × Liberal = −0.123, p = 0.032) reflects supply-side translation: in the Liberal regimes (Ireland, UK), exclusionary attitudes can't channel into radical right ballots because the radical right is structurally suppressed — Ireland has no significant RR party in this period, the UK channels exclusion through Brexit and mainstream-Conservative voting. The opposite signs aren't a contradiction of the attitude finding; they're what supply-side filtering looks like in the data. A complete account of attitude-to-vote translation would require party-level data and electoral-system features, which is properly a separate paper.
